sentences.text: Shall I tell the day exactly what-- the way-- the way it is now?
I was a little boy when he died.
Rivka must be born in 1906, '07, something like that.
He had some kind of-- all kind of business.
He was buying cattle and other stuff like that.
Really, exactly, I was a kid.
I don't know exactly what he was doing.
No, he wasn't going from town to town selling.
No, my mother just was doing-- taking care of the kids.
He was-- he left Poland, I think, in 1908.
He'd been in United States approximately for six years.
Just I saw the place when I come here, and I saw it.
Him with his brother, his brother Morris.
I have no idea why he come back.
I just remember he wanted to go back.
He-- he had from all kinds of companies, papers with boats and everything.
I wasn't even born at that time.
But we used to sleep-- it was a-- we were six boys and a girl.
We used to have two in a room, three in a room.
And we used to have a little dresser.
Yeah, I used to like soccer, play soccer.
With some non-Jewish boys, because the Jewish wasn't too many.
I've got to think about them.
I was fast and strong, and I was good.
Close was a-- a big place close to the church, to the Catholic church down there.
We used to play in schoolyards.
We used to celebrate Shabbos, I remember, when my father was alive.
We used to go to the shul, Shabbos and all of the holidays.
Only when we used to come back, we used to sing, zemiros and all kind of stuff, you know.
Like [INAUDIBLE] or some other songs, we used to sing with my father.
I think he used to sing nice.
My brother used to sing pretty good, my younger brother.
That time, I don't know exactly the voice, what-- my younger brother was very talented in-- musical.
Oh, like every Jewish mother, cholent, um, [INAUDIBLE], desserts.
Like every mother, some other Jewish-- like, food, what is challah, used to bake challah, everything, you know.
The shul was located a little bit-- outside the town.
I was little when I was in-- in Nacza.
When my father died, we used to go every day to shul to say Kaddish, my father.
I remember we always used to say Kaddish after my father.
When we been little, then always was a problem with a minyan.
When we been older, then it was a problem with a minyan on our kids was bar mitzvah.
We had half a minyan in our house.
Then they brought the Sefer Torah in our house.
And they used to make in our house the minyan.
It was easier to make a minyan.
And when we left, they say, meaning the ghetto-- they forced us, when the German came in, they forced us to go to the ghetto.
We took the Sefer Torah with us.
And we used to make a minyan during the-- when we was in the occupation-- in the ghetto.
Every day we used to make a minyan, and everything was [INAUDIBLE].
We have a shochet, not a rabbi.
I remember what-- he went like, I think, Purim to shul.
I don't know if he got sick.
And they took him to a hospital in Eishyshok.
And he been for-- for a while down there.
When he die, Pesach, the seventh day of Pesach was my-- it's yahrzeit.
I used to say, always yahrzeit.
Still, I'm saying Kaddish now after my father, the seventh day of Pesach.
My mother has a little store.

And naturally, my brothers, they used to work with some-- all in wood, and all kind of-- all kind of stuff to-- from-- from like, from the forest to the station, to load the-- I think it was loading to send it to Germany, the wood.
And I mostly was helping out our relatives from the United States.
My-- my father's brother, Morris, he used to help us out a lot.
And my grandfather, he used to daven in shul, like the holidays, on Passover.
And the money, he used to send to my mother, because my mother was a widow with seven kids.
And the money used to send for my mother.
The pay was very-- very, very little pay.
Every penny what I used to make, I give-- I give to my mother.
When I was-- when I was 12, I started on blacksmith's work.
He would just-- he have a slave labor.
That's how you-- the pay was very little.
Oh, I used to get up early and work late.
And the pay was very little.
Oh, I used to do all kind of work, farming work, and for farmers, and for-- shoe horses, and wagons, and all kind of work.
Mostly-- mostly at the times, farming.
Then I was in the army.
I was doing the same work, so a blacksmith.
Still, still I don't get paid.
Not very-- I was going to school, to public school, very little school.
I think I was seven when I went to school.
And I was going to school, and I was pretty good in school.
I was good at-- and then when my father died, my mother took us to Eishyshok, me and my younger brother.
I was eight, and he was six, I think.
And the older brother went around and learned in the [INAUDIBLE].
And we was going to cheder.
She-- she was strict, and she was a big anti-Semite.
She was strict, and she was big anti-Semite.
I know what this anti-Semite means.
Or she's-- the way she spoke to Jewish kids.
When I was a kid, I took any-- everything.
We got more than one grade.
It was-- in Eishyshok was a teacher, Shayel Lidzki.
I think he was in the war.
He was teaching-- they was teaching reading, writing.
After I get older, I learn [INAUDIBLE].
And with the board, we used-- eat teg.
Teg, they arrange for us, for me and my brother, every day to another family go to eat.
Like every Tuesday, to this person, we come to eat.
Like at Monday, to this person come to eat.
And some-- then when we bartered, people from the what we eat the teg, they used to give us a dollar, a zloty, Polish zloty.
It's enough for one day to eat, [? even ?] one we bartered for that.
Depends on-- some was pretty good, and some was not very good.
I have seven persons a week, seven balabustas, you know.
[LAUGHS] And then some one was not very good.
Just I don't have a choice.
Oh, I remember one name was [INAUDIBLE].
She was a very pretty girl.
And she was-- and she used to give me the food.
She was a very, very nice to me when she was giving it, very nice, the best food that you can get.
Oh, I used to get meat, everything.
On like Shabbos, they used to make [INAUDIBLE] and all kind of stuff, you know.
I used to come home for the holidays and for Pesach.
And this way, I used to be all summer there and all winter there, just for the holidays.
And then beginning another semester, I used to go back.
My mother is arrange again, every year.
My mother-- my older brother used to pick us up with horse and buggy.
She used to come, used to play and-- play with the kids together.
And I was not particular to the dishes.
I know my mother did the best way she could because she had a hard life.
When I work from the beginning, like with that Chaim David Schmidt.
And then I went in another town in Eishyshok.
Down there I get better paid.
And then I went-- from then, I went to Radun.
From Radun, I used to go every day with a bicycle, from Nacza to Radun, 11 kilometers.
And then Friday, he keep me long.
And then it comes in Shabbos, Friday night.
Then from the [INAUDIBLE] the rabbi, they find out.
They tried to stop my bus to keep me so long to not to go in [INAUDIBLE].
They was-- I heard it was stuck, and they did nothing.
I stayed for a few years.
And I was for quite a while without work.
They used to come to Eishyshok I was staying in Eishyshok.
And then the army, then I went to the army.
Yeah, I was-- I was drafted.
I tell you in Polish or in English?
The First Regiment Artillery-- Light Artillery Legionaries from Joseph [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know the first name.
I remember the second name, Lapicki.
No, it was a few more.
Kruvski, ?] a good friend of mine, [?
He was a very good friend of mine.
And there was some others I don't remember anymore.
When I was a recruit and I come  in in the army, and my brother-- was a list from the same regiment, in the same unit, exactly.

When I-- when I come in, and they brought me in the lines, and that officer was reading the list from their recruits, and he find my name, and he said, that's the same-- that's the brother from the same Asner what was over here?
I'll go by if I'll recognize you.
When he come to me, and he stopped, and he looked at me, he said, you Asner.
He said, you'll be like your brother, it will be all right.
